---
title: 'Secrets Management for the Workflow Construct'
subtitle: 'A three-tier scheme using pass (GPG-encrypted text files) for API keys, AWS IAM Identity Center for AWS credentials, and gitleaks at staging time as the residual safety net'
author: "Ronald 'Ryy' G. Thomas"
date: '2026-04-28'
categories: [setup, security, secrets, cloud, aws]
description: >
  An open-source secrets-management scheme for the Workflow
  Construct. The post documents the installation and
  configuration of pass (the Unix password store), the AWS
  IAM Identity Center workflow that replaces plaintext
  credentials, and the secret-injection patterns that keep
  API keys and tokens out of dotfiles, shell history, and
  the working tree. Open-source alternatives (gopass,
  bitwarden-cli with vaultwarden) are presented as
  conditional substitutes; the proprietary 1Password CLI is
  noted for users already paying for it but is not the
  recommended path.
image: 'media/images/hero.jpg'
document-type: 'blog'
draft: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
---

![A row of small brass keys hanging from labelled hooks on
a wooden board, each hook engraved with a single illegible
word. Beside the board, a closed strongbox with a worn
brass keyhole. The image evokes the idea that secrets are
state, that the state must be inventoried and labelled, and
that the inventory belongs in a different physical location
from the locks it
opens.](media/images/hero.jpg){.img-fluid width=80%}

*Secrets are state. They deserve the same backup, audit, and
recovery discipline as any other state in the workflow
construct.*

# Introduction

A working data scientist's laptop accumulates secrets the
way a cluttered desk accumulates pens: silently, in
multiple drawers, with no inventory of what is where. By
the time the laptop is two years old it holds an AWS
access-key pair in `~/.aws/credentials`, an Anthropic API
key in `~/.zshrc` exported as a shell variable, an
OpenAI key in a project-specific `.env` file, a Zotero API
token in a Quarto YAML, a GitHub personal-access token in
the keyring, an institutional VPN certificate in
`~/.vpn/`, and one or two SSH private keys in `~/.ssh/`.
Each was placed there in good faith by a past version of
the operator who had a specific task to complete and no
inclination to engineer a generic solution. The
accumulated state has no inventory, no rotation schedule,
no recovery plan if the laptop is lost, and no shared
abstraction across the construct's other layers.

The cost of this state of affairs is paid in three
predictable failure modes. First, secrets leak: an API key
exported in `.zshrc` is sourced into every subshell,
including the ones that scrape `env` for diagnostics, and
eventually a stack trace or a verbose-logging script
prints the variable verbatim. Second, secrets rot: a
long-lived AWS access key issued in 2023 is still in
`credentials` in 2026, with no automation reminding the
operator to rotate it, and no audit trail of which scripts
have used it in the meantime. Third, secrets are lost when
the laptop is: a fresh machine has no `~/.zshrc`, no
`~/.aws/credentials`, and no `~/.env` until the operator
manually reconstructs each from memory or from another
machine that has not yet been wiped.

This post documents an open-source three-tier secrets
scheme that addresses all three failure modes. The first
tier is a single encrypted store (`pass`, the Unix password
store) for long-lived API keys and tokens, synchronised
across machines via a private git remote. The second tier
is short-lived credentials for AWS, obtained via AWS IAM
Identity Center (formerly SSO), expiring automatically
without operator action. The third tier is the residual
safety net of `gitleaks`-at-staging, documented in
[post 49](/posts/49-zzgit/), which catches the cases where
the first two tiers have already failed.

More formally, this post documents an extension to the
Cloud and File-system layers of the Workflow Construct
described in [post 52](/posts/52-workflow-construct/),
specifically the secrets-management concern that cuts
across both. The construct's other layers all assume that
secrets are reachable when needed but invisible when not;
the scheme documented here is the operative artefact that
makes the assumption true. The recommendation is
deliberately open-source: the construct's other layers are
auditable down to the source, and the secrets layer should
be too. A non-open-source alternative (the proprietary
1Password CLI) is documented in an appendix for users who
already pay for it, but it is not the recommended path.

## Motivations

The pain points that motivated documenting this scheme are
specific:

- The laptop's `~/.aws/credentials` file holds the
  long-lived access keys for an IAM user with substantial
  permissions. The keys do not expire. If the laptop is
  stolen, the recovery procedure is not 'log into AWS and
  rotate', because the operator no longer has the laptop
  to log in from; it is 'log into AWS from a different
  machine, panic, rotate everything, audit the logs for
  unauthorised use'.
- API keys exported in `.zshrc` are visible to every
  process the user starts. A diagnostic script that runs
  `env | sort` will print the keys to its log; a
  collaborator who joins a screen-shared session will see
  them in `printenv` output; a CI runner that inherits the
  shell environment will receive them by accident.
- Multi-machine work (laptop, ThinkPad, EC2 instance, HPC
  cluster) creates pressure to copy `.aws/credentials`
  across machines, which doubles or triples the
  blast-radius of a single key compromise.
- The `zzgit` secret scanner ([post 49](/posts/49-zzgit/))
  catches accidentally-committed secrets at staging time,
  which is necessary but not sufficient: it depends on the
  user actually running `zzgit` rather than `git commit
  -am`, and it does not address the secrets that are
  correctly stored in the working tree's dotfiles but
  should not be there at all.
- The construct's other layers are open-source and
  auditable. A proprietary CLI for the secrets layer would
  be a foreign body in an otherwise transparent stack;
  particularly so given that the trust requirement on a
  secrets binary is exceptionally high: a single
  subverted code path could compromise every system the
  operator authenticates to.

## Objectives

This post sets out to deliver:

1. A reproducible installation and configuration of `pass`
   (the Unix password store) for non-AWS API keys and
   tokens, synchronised across machines via a private git
   remote.
2. A walk-through of AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO)
   configuration that replaces the long-lived
   `~/.aws/credentials` file with short-lived
   tokens obtained via `aws sso login`.
3. A small set of secret-injection patterns that move
   secrets from environment variables (visible to every
   process) to per-process injection (visible only to the
   process that needs them).
4. A failure-mode catalogue covering GPG-key loss,
   sync-conflict resolution, and the macOS-Keychain /
   gpg-agent interaction, plus a documented recovery
   procedure for a lost laptop.

The reader should be able to install and configure the
scheme in approximately two hours: thirty minutes for `pass`
and GPG, thirty minutes for AWS IAM Identity Center
configuration at the AWS-account level (one-time, requires
admin access), and an hour for the per-tool migrations and
verification.

![Placeholder ambiance image (coffee, Unsplash free
licence). To be replaced with a screenshot of `pass ls`
output showing a small organised hierarchy of API keys
under category headings (anthropic/, openai/, github/,
etc.).](media/images/placeholder-coffee-02.jpg){.img-fluid}

# What Is the Scheme?

The scheme has three tiers, distinguished by the
characteristics of the secrets they hold:

## Tier 1: long-lived API keys and tokens (`pass`)

Most non-AWS secrets are long-lived API keys: the
Anthropic key issued at signup, the OpenAI key minted from
the dashboard, the GitHub personal-access token created
last quarter for a CI pipeline, the Zotero API token
copied from the user-preferences page. They have no
automatic expiration, are reissued infrequently, and are
re-used across multiple invocations.

The `pass` store, also known as the Unix password store,
is the canonical open-source home for them. It is a thin
shell wrapper around GPG: each secret is a small text
file encrypted with a user GPG key, stored in
`~/.password-store/`, and optionally synchronised to a
private git remote. The interface is a small set of
verbs: `pass insert anthropic/api-key` to add, `pass
anthropic/api-key` to retrieve, `pass git push` to sync.
The implementation is approximately 700 lines of bash; the
audit surface is small and the behaviour is predictable.

## Tier 2: short-lived cloud credentials (AWS IAM Identity Center)

AWS credentials are different in kind from API keys: they
authorise broad permissions and are best replaced rather
than guarded. AWS IAM Identity Center (the service
formerly known as AWS SSO) issues short-lived tokens by
default, configurable per-user, with eight-hour sessions
as the conventional default. The tokens are obtained via
`aws sso login` and stored under
`~/.aws/sso/cache/` rather than in `~/.aws/credentials`;
they expire automatically and re-authentication is a
single command.

The construct treats AWS as the canonical example of this
tier; other cloud providers (Google Cloud, Azure) have
analogous short-lived-credential mechanisms which are not
covered here but follow the same shape.

## Tier 3: residual safety net (`gitleaks` at staging)

Tiers 1 and 2 prevent the most common ways secrets reach
the working tree: API keys go to `pass`, AWS credentials
go to short-lived caches, neither is exported in
`.zshrc`. Despite this, secrets occasionally slip through:
a credential pasted into a notebook output cell, an SSH
private key copied into a project directory for testing,
a `.env` file that was supposed to be gitignored but was
not. The `gitleaks`-at-staging integration documented in
[post 49](/posts/49-zzgit/) is the residual safety net
that catches these. It is a separate post and is not
documented here beyond cross-reference; the present post's
contribution is the upstream prevention work that reduces
the volume of cases that reach the safety net.

# Prerequisites

The scheme assumes:

- **GPG key.** A user GPG key is required for `pass`. Users
  who already sign git commits with GPG can reuse the
  existing key; users without one can generate a fresh key
  during installation. The key is the single point of
  failure for the entire encrypted store and warrants
  careful backup (see Things to Watch Out For below).
- **AWS account with IAM Identity Center enabled.** The
  identity-center configuration is a one-time, AWS
  account-level setup that requires admin permissions. For
  personal AWS accounts the setup is straightforward; for
  institutional accounts the user may need to coordinate
  with an administrator.
- **Existing construct setup.** The dotfiles repository
  ([post 24](/posts/24-setupdotfilesongithub/)) and the
  zsh setup ([post 01](/posts/01-configtermzsh/)) should
  be in place. The scheme adds a small block to `.zshrc`
  and a `~/.password-store/` directory.
- **Private git remote for `pass` sync.** A private
  repository on GitHub, GitLab, or a self-hosted
  forge. The pass-store contents are GPG-encrypted before
  being committed, but the existence of an inventory of
  encrypted secrets is itself sometimes a privacy concern
  worth keeping behind authentication.
- **Time investment.** Approximately two hours for the
  full setup, dominated by the AWS IAM Identity Center
  configuration (one-time, ~30 minutes) and the migration
  of existing secrets from `.zshrc` and `.env` files into
  `pass` (~60 minutes for a moderate-sized inventory).

# Installation

## Tier 1: `pass` and GPG

The `pass` binary is small (a single bash script plus a
manpage) and available in every major package manager.

```sh
# macOS
brew install pass gnupg

# Debian-derived Linux
sudo apt install pass gnupg

# Confirm both are present
pass version
gpg --version | head -1
```

If a GPG key already exists (for git commit signing or
otherwise), reuse it; the long-form key ID is the input
to `pass init`:

```sh
gpg --list-secret-keys --keyid-format=long
# /Users/zenn/.gnupg/pubring.kbx
# ------------------------------
# sec   rsa4096/ABCD1234EFGH5678 2024-01-15 [SC]
#       FULL-FINGERPRINT-HERE
# uid           [ultimate] Ronald 'Ryy' G. Thomas <user@example.com>
# ssb   rsa4096/9876ZYXW5432VUTS 2024-01-15 [E]

pass init ABCD1234EFGH5678
# mkdir: created directory '/Users/zenn/.password-store/'
# Password store initialized for ABCD1234EFGH5678
```

If no GPG key exists, generate one before initialising
`pass`:

```sh
gpg --full-generate-key
# Choose: (1) RSA and RSA, 4096 bits, no expiration, real name,
# email matching git config, comment optional, strong passphrase.
# After generation:
gpg --list-secret-keys --keyid-format=long
pass init <new-key-id>
```

Initialise the git remote for cross-machine sync. The
remote should be a private repository because it contains
the encrypted-secret filenames (the contents are
encrypted, but the inventory is metadata):

```sh
cd ~/.password-store
pass git init
pass git remote add origin git@github.com:mygit/password-store.git
pass git push -u origin master
```

## Tier 2: AWS IAM Identity Center

The IAM Identity Center setup is a one-time AWS-account
operation. The high-level steps are:

1. In the AWS console, enable IAM Identity Center for the
   account.
2. Create a permission set (the named bundle of
   permissions a session will receive).
3. Assign the permission set to the user (the operator's
   identity) for the appropriate AWS account.

These three steps are AWS-console-only and depend on the
account's current state; AWS's documentation (see See
Also) is the canonical reference. After the AWS-side setup
is complete, the laptop-side configuration is a single
command:

```sh
aws configure sso
# SSO start URL: https://my-org.awsapps.com/start
# SSO Region: us-west-1
# (a browser window opens; the operator authenticates;
#  the CLI receives a session token)
# CLI default client Region [None]: us-west-1
# CLI default output format [None]: json
# CLI profile name [123456789012_PowerUser]: my-profile
```

Subsequent re-authentication is one command:

```sh
aws sso login --profile my-profile
# (browser opens; operator authenticates; new token is cached)
```

The legacy `~/.aws/credentials` file should be deleted
after IAM Identity Center is configured and verified, so
that the long-lived access keys are no longer reachable:

```sh
mv ~/.aws/credentials ~/.aws/credentials.legacy.bak
# After verifying that all scripts work via the SSO profile,
# rotate the legacy keys via the AWS console and remove the
# backup:
# rm ~/.aws/credentials.legacy.bak
```

# Configuration

## `pass` daily use

The `.zshrc` block to make `pass`-stored secrets reachable
on demand is small. The construct convention is to inject
secrets at process-start time rather than to export them
into the shell environment, so that they are visible only
to the process that needs them.

```zsh
# ============================================================
# Secrets injection helpers (post 55)
# ============================================================

# Inject a single pass-stored secret as an environment
# variable for the duration of one command. Usage:
#   with-secret VAR_NAME pass/path command...
# Example:
#   with-secret ANTHROPIC_API_KEY anthropic/api-key claude
with-secret() {
  local var_name="$1"
  local pass_path="$2"
  shift 2
  local value
  value="$(pass "$pass_path")" || {
    echo "with-secret: pass lookup failed for '$pass_path'" >&2
    return 1
  }
  env "$var_name=$value" "$@"
}

# Convenience wrappers for the most-used secret-bearing tools.
# Each wraps the underlying tool with with-secret so the
# operator does not have to remember the pass path.
claude() {
  with-secret ANTHROPIC_API_KEY anthropic/api-key \
    command claude "$@"
}

# Add similar wrappers for openai, gh, etc., as needed.
```

The `with-secret` pattern is preferred over exporting:

```zsh
# WRONG: visible to every subprocess for the rest of the session
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$(pass anthropic/api-key)
claude

# RIGHT: visible only to the wrapped command
with-secret ANTHROPIC_API_KEY anthropic/api-key claude
```

## GPG agent caching

`pass` invokes `gpg` for each retrieval, which prompts for
the GPG-key passphrase. Caching the passphrase in
`gpg-agent` avoids the repeated prompt while limiting
exposure to a configurable cache lifetime.

The `~/.gnupg/gpg-agent.conf` file:

```ini
# ~/.gnupg/gpg-agent.conf
default-cache-ttl 3600    # 1 hour
max-cache-ttl 28800       # 8 hours
pinentry-program /opt/homebrew/bin/pinentry-mac   # macOS
# pinentry-program /usr/bin/pinentry-curses       # Linux
```

After editing the config, reload the agent:

```sh
gpg-connect-agent reloadagent /bye
```

## AWS IAM Identity Center: profile usage

Once configured, the SSO profile is used the same way as
any AWS profile:

```sh
# Single command via profile
aws s3 ls --profile my-profile

# Or set the profile for the session
export AWS_PROFILE=my-profile
aws s3 ls

# Or per-script via env
AWS_PROFILE=my-profile aws s3 ls
```

The token is cached at `~/.aws/sso/cache/` and refreshed
automatically by the SDK as long as the cached token is
not yet expired. When it expires, the next AWS CLI call
emits an authentication error and the operator runs
`aws sso login --profile my-profile` to refresh.

# Verification

```sh
# Tier 1: pass
pass version
pass init                                  # confirms a key is set
pass ls                                    # lists current store contents
echo 'verify-only' | pass insert -m verify-temp/test
pass verify-temp/test                      # should print 'verify-only'
pass rm verify-temp/test
pass git status                            # confirms remote sync state

# Tier 2: AWS SSO
aws sts get-caller-identity --profile my-profile
# Should return the assumed-role ARN, not an IAM user ARN

ls -la ~/.aws/credentials 2>&1
# Should error 'No such file or directory' after migration

# Tier 3: gitleaks (cross-reference post 49)
gitleaks version
which gz                                   # the construct's git wizard
```

A green pass on all four blocks indicates the scheme is
materialised. A failure on `pass git status` localises to
a sync issue; a failure on `aws sts get-caller-identity`
localises to a token expiration (run `aws sso login`); a
failure on `gitleaks` is the residual-safety-net concern
addressed in post 49.

# Daily Workflow

| Task | Command | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Add a new API key | `pass insert anthropic/api-key` | Prompts for the value (not echoed) |
| Add a multi-line secret (e.g., a private key) | `pass insert -m ssh/research-key` | Use `-m` for multi-line input |
| Retrieve a secret to stdout | `pass anthropic/api-key` | Avoid in pipelines that log; prefer `with-secret` |
| Inject into a command | `with-secret VAR pass/path command` | Visible only to `command`'s process tree |
| List current store | `pass ls` | Shows hierarchy of stored secrets |
| Search by name | `pass find anthropic` | Greps the store names |
| Sync to remote | `pass git push` | Encrypted; safe over public network |
| Pull from remote (new machine) | `pass git pull` | Requires the GPG private key already present |
| AWS session refresh | `aws sso login --profile my-profile` | Browser-based; fast |
| AWS-using command | `AWS_PROFILE=my-profile aws ...` | Token resolved automatically |

The `with-secret` wrapper is the load-bearing daily-use
verb. Once the construct is installed, almost every
secret-bearing command (Claude Code, the GitHub CLI, the
OpenAI CLI, custom Python or R scripts) is invoked via
`with-secret`; the underlying secret never leaves
`~/.password-store/` decrypted on disk for longer than the
process's lifetime.

![Placeholder ambiance image (coffee, Unsplash free
licence). To be replaced with a screenshot of `aws sso
login` opening a browser, with the CLI showing the
'Successfully logged in' message after the browser
flow.](media/images/placeholder-coffee-03.jpg){.img-fluid}

# Things to Watch Out For

Six gotchas dominate the failure modes of this scheme.
Each is small in isolation; in aggregation they are the
reasons users abandon `pass` in the first month.

- **The GPG private key is the single point of failure for
  the entire pass store.** If the key is lost, every
  encrypted secret in `~/.password-store/` is permanently
  unreadable. Back up the GPG key to two physical locations
  (one off-site) before adding any secret of consequence.
  The backup procedure is `gpg --export-secret-keys
  --armor <key-id> > gpg-private-key.asc`; store the
  resulting file on an encrypted USB drive in a locked
  drawer and a second copy with a trusted off-site party
  (or a safe-deposit box).
- **The `pass` git remote contains encrypted secrets but a
  plain-text inventory of names.** Pushing the store to a
  public repository would expose to the world that the
  operator has, for example, an `internal-systems/db-prod-
  password` entry. The contents are encrypted and the name
  is metadata, but the metadata is itself sensitive in
  some contexts. The remote should be a private
  repository, ideally on a forge that enforces
  authentication for both reads and writes.
- **macOS Keychain and `gpg-agent` can fight over
  `pinentry`.** Symptom: a passphrase prompt appears as a
  Keychain dialog rather than a `gpg-agent` prompt, or
  vice versa, and the wrong one captures the input. Fix:
  set `pinentry-program /opt/homebrew/bin/pinentry-mac` in
  `~/.gnupg/gpg-agent.conf`, restart the agent, and ensure
  `GPG_TTY=$(tty)` is exported in `.zshrc` so terminal-
  driven `gpg` commands route to the right pinentry.
- **AWS SSO sessions expire silently from the CLI's
  perspective.** A long-running script that issues an AWS
  call after the cached token has expired sees an
  authentication error rather than a refresh prompt. The
  fix is operational: scripts that run for longer than the
  configured session duration (default eight hours) should
  either chunk their work into per-token batches or use a
  `--role-session-name` and assume-role chain that the
  SDK can refresh transparently.
- **Empty store after fresh install.** A first `pass ls`
  on a freshly-cloned `~/.password-store` reports nothing
  even when the operator expects content. The cause is
  usually that the new machine does not yet have the GPG
  private key imported; `pass` silently skips entries it
  cannot decrypt rather than warning. Run `gpg --list-
  secret-keys` to confirm the key is present; import via
  `gpg --import gpg-private-key.asc` if not.
- **Secrets exported in `.zshrc` continue to work even
  after `pass` is set up.** A migration that adds `pass`
  but does not remove the legacy `export FOO=...` lines
  results in two copies of each secret coexisting, with
  `.zshrc` taking precedence because it runs first. Audit
  `.zshrc` for `export.*KEY|export.*TOKEN|export.*SECRET`
  patterns and replace each with a `with-secret` wrapper
  before considering the migration complete.

# Uninstall / Rollback

```sh
# Tier 1: remove pass
brew uninstall pass               # macOS
# or apt remove pass              # Debian
rm -rf ~/.password-store          # CAUTION: irrecoverable

# Tier 2: revert to long-lived AWS credentials
mv ~/.aws/credentials.legacy.bak ~/.aws/credentials
# Remove the SSO profile from ~/.aws/config

# .zshrc: remove the with-secret block and any wrapper
# functions added (claude, openai, etc.). Restore any legacy
# 'export FOO=...' lines from version control.

# GPG: optional; leave intact if used for git commit signing
```

The migration is reversible per tier, but Tier 1's
removal step (`rm -rf ~/.password-store`) is destructive
and irreversible without the GPG-key backup. If the goal
is to stop using `pass` while preserving the secrets,
export them first via `pass git push` (to ensure the
remote is current), then archive the local store
(`mv ~/.password-store ~/password-store-archive-2026-04-28`)
rather than deleting outright.

![Placeholder ambiance image (coffee, Unsplash free
licence). To be replaced with a screenshot of a `with-
secret` wrapper invocation showing the secret being
injected and the command output, with no trace of the
secret in `env` or shell
history.](media/images/placeholder-coffee-04.jpg){.img-fluid}

# Lessons Learnt

The migration on three machines (the construct's MacBook,
the ThinkPad with Linux Mint, and a clean EC2 Ubuntu
instance) surfaced lessons grouped into three buckets.

### Conceptual

- **Secrets are state, with the same lifecycle concerns as
  any other state.** They have an authoritative source,
  they need backup, they require recovery procedures, and
  they should not be silently duplicated. Treating them as
  state rather than as configuration is the conceptual
  shift that this scheme operationalises.
- **Short-lived credentials are strictly preferable to
  long-lived ones, where the issuer supports them.** AWS
  IAM Identity Center is the canonical example: an
  eight-hour token that re-authenticates from a SAML
  identity provider has a smaller blast-radius than a
  long-lived access key, and the user-experience cost of
  re-authentication is small once the workflow is
  habitual.
- **Open-source primitives are appropriate for the secrets
  layer.** The construct's other layers are auditable down
  to the source. The secrets layer holds the keys to
  every other system the operator relies on, which makes
  it the layer where source-code transparency matters
  most. Adopting a proprietary CLI here would be a
  category mismatch.
- **Per-process injection beats environment-variable
  export.** A secret exported in `.zshrc` is visible to
  every subprocess for the session's lifetime; a secret
  injected via `with-secret` is visible only to the
  wrapped command's process tree. The user-experience
  difference is negligible (a four-character prefix per
  command); the security difference is meaningful.

### Technical

- **`pass` is a thin wrapper over GPG, and the limits of
  the wrapper are the limits of GPG.** Multi-recipient
  encryption (sharing a secret with a collaborator) works
  but requires both parties' GPG keys; key rotation
  (re-encrypting the store with a new key) is a documented
  but non-trivial operation; offline access requires a
  GPG-agent cache, not just `pass` itself. None of this is
  surprising; it is worth knowing before adoption.
- **The pass-store git history is itself an audit log.**
  `pass git log` reveals when each secret was added or
  changed, which is occasionally useful for debugging
  ('why did this script start failing?' becomes 'when did
  the API key change?'). The audit log is not tamper-
  evident, but it is a reasonable starting point.
- **AWS IAM Identity Center sessions can be extended.**
  The default eight-hour session is configurable up to
  twelve hours per the AWS documentation, with longer
  sessions requiring a session-policy condition. For
  long-running batch jobs, the right pattern is an
  assume-role chain rather than a single long session.
- **`gpg-agent`'s caching behaviour differs subtly between
  macOS and Linux.** macOS with `pinentry-mac` integrates
  with the Keychain for the pinentry passphrase entry;
  Linux with `pinentry-curses` or `pinentry-gtk` does not.
  The construct's per-platform `.zshrc` block (post 01)
  should select the appropriate pinentry program.

### Gotcha-shaped

- **`pass git push` after `pass insert` does not happen
  automatically.** Each new secret must be explicitly
  pushed to the remote, or the new-machine recovery will
  miss the most recent additions. Adding an alias `alias
  passp='pass git push'` and developing the muscle memory
  is the simplest mitigation; an `insert`-then-`push`
  hook is possible but adds complexity.
- **`with-secret` does not pass through stdin
  transparently.** If the wrapped command reads from
  stdin, the redirection works as expected because
  `with-secret` is a shell function, not a wrapper
  process. However, `with-secret` does not pass through
  `tty` settings, which means commands that detect 'is my
  stdin a terminal?' may behave differently inside the
  wrapper. The cases are rare but real; a `claude --no-
  pager` analogue may be needed.
- **AWS IAM Identity Center's session cache is per-CLI-
  install, not per-OS-user.** A second CLI installation
  (e.g., a Homebrew-managed `awscli` plus a Python venv
  `awscli`) will maintain separate caches at different
  paths and a `login` against one will not authenticate
  the other. The fix is to standardise on one CLI install
  per machine.

# Limitations

The scheme as documented has the following honest
limitations:

- **It depends on GPG, which has its own complexity.**
  GPG-key generation, backup, rotation, and revocation
  are non-trivial topics, and `pass`'s usability is
  upper-bounded by GPG's. Users who find GPG operationally
  burdensome will find `pass` operationally burdensome by
  inheritance; the alternatives section below discusses
  this.
- **AWS IAM Identity Center requires admin access to
  configure at the account level.** Personal AWS accounts
  are easy; institutional accounts may require a
  conversation with an administrator who is unfamiliar
  with the construct's needs.
- **No hardware-token integration in this revision.**
  YubiKey-mediated GPG smart-card mode (the GPG key lives
  on the YubiKey rather than the laptop) is supported by
  GPG and `pass` but is a non-trivial setup that warrants
  its own follow-up post.
- **No coverage of TOTP / 2FA codes.** `pass-otp` is a
  popular extension that stores TOTP seeds alongside
  passwords; the present post does not cover it but the
  pattern is straightforward and is on the opportunities
  list below.
- **The recovery procedure for a lost laptop assumes the
  GPG-key backup is reachable.** If both the laptop and
  the USB-encrypted backup are lost simultaneously (e.g.,
  the laptop bag contains both), the recovery path is
  long: contact each issuer, prove identity, request a key
  rotation. The off-site backup mitigation in Things to
  Watch Out For is the principal defence.
- **The scheme is single-user.** Team-scope secret sharing
  (where a collaborator should be able to read a subset of
  the store) is supported by `pass` via multi-recipient
  encryption but is not covered here. The team-scope case
  is addressed by `gopass` (covered in the alternatives
  section) and by enterprise tools (Vault, Doppler) that
  are out of scope for the construct's single-user
  framing.

# Alternatives

`pass` is the recommended primary, but three alternatives
deserve mention.

## `gopass`: a `pass`-compatible Go reimplementation

`gopass` is a drop-in replacement for `pass` written in Go
rather than bash. It accepts the same `~/.password-store/`
layout, uses the same GPG-encryption pattern, and is
command-line compatible. The case for `gopass` over `pass`
is multi-store support (separate stores per team, per
client, etc.) and a slightly faster start-up time. The
case against is the larger binary footprint and the loss
of the `pass`-as-bash-script auditability that some users
value. For solo use, `pass` is sufficient; for users
already managing multiple `pass`-compatible stores,
`gopass` is a reasonable upgrade.

## `bitwarden-cli` against self-hosted `vaultwarden`

The Bitwarden ecosystem includes an open-source CLI
(`bw`) and an open-source server reimplementation
(Vaultwarden, formerly `bitwarden_rs`). The combination
gives a 1Password-like UX (web interface, browser
extension, mobile apps) without the proprietary
dependency: the server runs on the construct's own EC2
instance or local network, and the CLI is open-source. The
case for this path over `pass` is the user-experience
quality of the browser extensions and the multi-device
support; the case against is the operational cost of
running a server (backups, TLS, updates) and the larger
trust surface relative to `pass`'s small bash script.

## 1Password CLI (proprietary)

The 1Password CLI (`op`) is a closed-source binary
distributed by AgileBits under a commercial subscription.
For users already paying for 1Password and using its
desktop application, `op` integrates with the existing
vault and provides a comparable injection pattern (`op
run` is structurally similar to `with-secret`). The
construct does not recommend `op` as the primary because:

1. The binary is closed-source. The trust surface for the
   secrets layer warrants source-code visibility, which
   `op` does not provide.
2. The dependency is a paid subscription. The construct's
   other layers are zero-cost; secrets management should
   be too.
3. The data is held in AgileBits's hosted vault by
   default. Self-hosted alternatives exist but are not
   the canonical 1Password configuration.

For users who already pay for 1Password and accept the
above trade-offs, `op run -- command` is the
`with-secret`-equivalent invocation pattern. The migration
from `op` to `pass` is straightforward (`op item list` plus
a script that pipes each into `pass insert`), and is worth
considering if the proprietary dependency becomes
problematic.

# Opportunities for Improvement

Several extensions are plausible for subsequent revisions:

1. **`pass-otp` for TOTP integration.** The `pass-otp`
   extension stores TOTP seeds alongside passwords, with a
   `pass otp service-name` invocation that produces a
   current six-digit code. Eliminates the dependency on a
   separate authenticator app for services that the
   construct uses programmatically.
2. **YubiKey-mediated GPG smart-card mode.** Moves the GPG
   private key from `~/.gnupg/` to the YubiKey, so that
   `pass` decryption requires the physical token. Defends
   against laptop theft at the cost of token-loss
   recovery procedure complexity.
3. **`pass-import` for migration from other stores.**
   Imports from 1Password, LastPass, KeePass, Bitwarden,
   and others. Useful for users adopting `pass` after
   accumulating secrets in another tool.
4. **A construct-aware secrets recovery script.** A
   `~/bin/recover-secrets.sh` that walks a fresh laptop
   through GPG-key import, `pass git clone`, and the AWS
   SSO setup in sequence, with explicit checkpoints and
   rollback paths.
5. **A team-scope `gopass` follow-up.** Documents the
   multi-store, multi-recipient pattern for secrets that
   a small team needs to share, with explicit rotation
   procedures for when a member leaves.
6. **Hardware-token-backed AWS IAM Identity Center.**
   Configures IAM Identity Center to require a YubiKey-
   produced WebAuthn credential at login, rather than a
   password plus TOTP. Reduces the credential-phishing
   blast-radius for the AWS layer specifically.

# Wrapping Up

A laptop accumulates secrets the way a desk accumulates
pens: silently, in multiple drawers, with no inventory.
The accumulated state is the proximate cause of most
real-world credential incidents, not because individual
secrets are stored insecurely but because the operator
loses track of which secrets are where, and consequently
neither rotates them on schedule nor revokes them on
laptop loss.

The three-tier scheme documented here addresses the
inventory problem directly: an encrypted, GPG-keyed,
git-synchronised store (`pass`) for long-lived API keys;
short-lived tokens via AWS IAM Identity Center for cloud
credentials; and `gitleaks`-at-staging as the residual
safety net. Each tier has a small, auditable
implementation; each is built on open-source primitives;
each is reversible without losing the underlying secrets.

The recommendation against the proprietary 1Password CLI
is not a judgement of 1Password's product quality, which
is high. It is a judgement of category fit: the secrets
layer is the layer where source-code transparency matters
most, because the trust requirement is roughly 'I bet my
entire research programme on this'. The construct is built
on auditable primitives at every other layer, and the
secrets layer should not be the exception.

### Main takeaways

- **Treat secrets as state.** Inventory, backup, rotate,
  recover. The same disciplines that apply to research
  data apply to the keys that unlock the systems holding
  the data.
- **Prefer short-lived credentials where the issuer
  supports them.** AWS IAM Identity Center is the
  canonical example; the same pattern applies to other
  cloud providers.
- **Inject per-process; do not export.** A secret in
  `.zshrc` is visible to every subprocess. A secret
  injected via `with-secret` is visible only to the
  command that needs it.
- **Choose open-source primitives at the secrets layer.**
  The trust requirement is high enough that source
  visibility is load-bearing.

# See Also

- Posts in this repository at adjacent layers:
  - [post 22](/posts/22-serversetupawscli/): the AWS CLI
    setup that this post complements (and that requires
    revision once IAM Identity Center is in place).
  - [post 23](/posts/23-serversetupawsconsole/): the AWS
    console walkthrough; same revision note as 22.
  - [post 24](/posts/24-setupdotfilesongithub/): the
    workstation-IaC keystone where the `with-secret`
    helper and any wrapper functions live.
  - [post 25](/posts/25-setupgit/): the version-control
    layer that GPG commit signing lives alongside.
  - [post 49](/posts/49-zzgit/): the staging-time
    secret scanner that complements the prevention work
    documented here.
  - [post 52](/posts/52-workflow-construct/): the
    construct framing that names the secrets-management
    concern this post addresses.

- Project repositories and references:
  - `pass`: [https://www.passwordstore.org/](https://www.passwordstore.org/)
  - `gopass`: [https://github.com/gopasspw/gopass](https://github.com/gopasspw/gopass)
  - `bitwarden-cli`: [https://bitwarden.com/help/cli/](https://bitwarden.com/help/cli/)
  - Vaultwarden: [https://github.com/dani-garcia/vaultwarden](https://github.com/dani-garcia/vaultwarden)
  - `pass-otp`: [https://github.com/tadfisher/pass-otp](https://github.com/tadfisher/pass-otp)
  - GnuPG: [https://gnupg.org/](https://gnupg.org/)
  - AWS IAM Identity Center user guide:
    [https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/)
  - AWS CLI v2 SSO configuration:
    [https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/sso-configure-profile-token.html](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/sso-configure-profile-token.html)

# Reproducibility

The configuration was developed and verified on the
following software stack:

| Component | Version | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Operating system | macOS 15 (Sequoia) | primary daily driver |
| Operating system | Linux Mint 22 (Wilma) | secondary verification |
| Operating system | Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (EC2) | clean-room verification |
| Shell | zsh 5.9 | minimum 5.0 |
| `pass` | 1.7.4 | minimum 1.7 |
| GnuPG | 2.4.5 | minimum 2.2 |
| `pinentry-mac` | 1.3.0 | macOS only |
| AWS CLI | 2.18 | minimum 2.13 for SSO token-cache changes |
| Optional | `gopass` 1.15 | conditional alternative |
| Optional | `bitwarden-cli` 2024.10 | conditional alternative |
| Optional | `vaultwarden` 1.32 | self-hosted Bitwarden server |
| Recommended companion | `gitleaks` 8.21 | post 49 staging scanner |

Date of last verification: 2026-04-28.

# Feedback

Corrections, suggestions, and questions are welcome. Please
open an issue or pull request on the
[GitHub repository](https://github.com/mygit) or send an
email to user@example.com. The most useful contributions
are platform-specific deviations (the GPG-agent
configuration on freshly-installed Linux distributions
varies more than the post's verification matrix captures)
and migration recipes from other stores into `pass`.
